hypothesis: If the β parameter in DPO controls the degree of distributional shift from the SFT policy, then dynamically scheduling β across training—starting high (conservative, near-SFT) and annealing it downward as the model's reward improves—will simultaneously prevent the reward-hacking collapse observed in OLMo and achieve higher mean reward than fixed-β DPO in LLaMA and Gemma, because early-stage conservatism preserves the chemistry grammar learned during SFT while late-stage permissiveness allows greater exploitation of high-reward compositional regions.
